components.templateData:
Background

What is a castle?

Definition

This site is about "real" medieval castles, synonym for strongholds, in German Burg, in French Château fort and in Dutch burcht.

To be able to make out the real castles we need a good definition. The book "1000 jaar kastelen in Nederland" [1] gives a useful definition of a castle:

"A castle is a medieval building that combines defensive with domestic functions by providing a living space and a defence to a limited group of persons differing of a noble family with a few servants to a small court and a military garrison up to a maximum of approximately fifty persons. Essential hereby is that this group depends on a relation to one person or institution."

Summarised, a real castle meets these criteria:

  • It is built in the middle ages (appr. 500–1500)
  • It provides a living space to a limited group of persons
  • It provides a defence to a limited group of persons
  • The group depends on a relation to one person or institution

City castles

A special case is the city castle. When is such a castle qualified as castle and when as a walled city? Sir Oman [2] gives a useful definition of a city castle:

"A castle is a military structure larger than a tower but smaller than a fortified town, it is residential but is also defensible in character. However complicated internally it must be a complete unit unto itself and not part of a town. A castle may exist inside a town but must be able to be cut off by the closing of a gate or the raising of a drawbridge."

To be qualified as a castle, a city castle must be able to defend itself independently of the city and the city walls. Two famous examples:

Which castles do not qualify?

Categories

Some famous castles do not qualify following the definition. Four categories:

  • Renaissance-, romantic- and new castles
  • Imperial, royal and noble palaces
  • Fortifications
  • Walled cities

Renaissance-, romantic- and new castles

After the invention of gun powder many castles lost their defensive function. New castles were built with the emphasis on comfort and splendour. These renaissance castles often have the ground plan of a rectangular castle but the structure is not based on defense.

Examples of renaissance castles:

  • Château de Chambord (France)
  • Château de Chenonceaux (France)
  • Château de d'Ussé (France)

Romantic castles are built in the 19th century and reflect a nostalgic longing to an idealised image of the medieval court life:

  • Schloß Neuschwanstein (Germany)
  • Schloß Liechtenstein (Germany)
  • Balmoral Castle (Scotland)

Imperial, royal and noble palaces

The emperors of the holy roman empire but also kings and other noblemen built palaces ("Pfalzen") that had residential, administrative or representative function, but did not have a defensive function.

  • The Ridderzaal of the Binnenhof in the Hague (Netherlands)
  • Pfalz Goslar (Germany)
  • Westminster Hall (England)

Fortifications

Forts and bastions are in fact defensive outposts of a city or an area. They are built mainly after the middle ages and are not inhabited like a castle.

  • St. Mawes Castle (England)
  • Pendennis Castle (England)
  • Agra Fort (India)
  • Lal Qilah (Red Fort, India)

Walled cities

Walled cities include city gates, watch towers and fortified bridges.

  • Citadel of Cairo (Egypt)
  • Mont Saint Michel (France)
  • The city walls of Constantinople (Istanbul, Turkey)

Literature

Books

  • [1] Janssen, H.L. e.a., "1000 jaar kastelen in Nederland", Utrecht, 1996
  • [2] Oman, C., Sir, "Castles", New York, 1978

Attempt to create a neutral rating for a castle top 100

What makes a castle a Top Castle? First: The "charisma" of a castle, a combination of its strategic location, through which the castle appears to dominate the landscape, the design that pairs beauty to an awe inspiring deterrence and the used materials through which the castle appears to dissolve its surroundings. Ditches with drawbridges, towers with battlements, halls with crosses and swords and dungeons and secret courses stand symbol for jurisdiction and protection but also for oppression and cruelty. A top castle recalls memories of sieges and conquests, famous inhabitants and prisoners, destruction and reconstruction and affirms its myths and ghost stories.

The proposition of topcastles.com is that the extent in which a castle meets this description is proportional to the attention that castle gets in books about castles and on the internet. A beautiful castle, a turbulent history or a remarkable military architecture inspires authors and photographers. In a nutshell, a top castle can be measured by the amount of stories and photographs it appears in. The authors and editors of books and websites make a virtual jury through their selection of castles and photographs and lay the basis for this castle top 100.

Calculation of the top 100 scores

Topcastles.com uses two ratings to calculate the top 100 score:

  • The appearance of the castle in specific sources
  • The rating of the castle given by visitors of topcastles.com

The final score of a castle is the sum of the sources score plus the visitors rating.

Calculation of the sources scores

The scores of the castles top 100 is calculated by simply counting how often a castle is described in books about castles (approximately 40 books, they generate 70% of the entire score).

Calculation of the score for the Visitors Top 100

Each castle gets an initial score of 5. Upon the first score the average rating is calculated. A first score of 10 results then in a rating of (5+10)/2 = 7.5. The average score is recalculated automatically on every vote.

Calculation of the total score

On January 1st of each year the visitor rating of a castle is added to the sources score to calculate the new top 100 for that year. Relatively in the end score, the sources score weighs twice as much as the visitors rating.

Castle books used to calculate the sources scores

* = Added in 2010

General castle books

  • Coppin, B., "Castles, Defending King and Country", Harpenden, 2006
  • Zeune, J., "Burgen, Symbole der Macht", Regensburg, 1997
  • Böhme, H. W. e.a., "Burgen in Mitteleuropa", Stuttgart, 1999
  • Schüerl, H., "Burgen und Schlösser in Deutschland", Frankfurt am Main, 1994
  • Brice, M., "Forts and Fortresses", London, 1990
  • Piper, O., "Burgenkunde", München, 1967
  • Toy, S., "Castles, their construction and history", New York, 1985
  • Oggins, R. S., "Castles and fortresses", New York, 1995
  • Kemp, A., "Castles in colour", Dorset, 1977
  • Paluzíe, A., "Great medieval castles of Spain", Barcelona, 2002
  • Hindley, G., "Castles of Europe", London, 1968
  • Ebhardt, B., "Der Wehrbau Europas im Mittelalter", Berlin, 1939
  • Schuchhardt, C., "Die Burg im Wandel der Weltgeschichte", Wildpark-Potsdam, 1931
  • Brown, R. Allen, "Castles, a history and guide", Poole, 1980
  • Gravett, C., "Castle", London (DK eyewitness), 2000
  • Gravett, C., "The History of Castles", Guilford, 2001
  • Libál, D., "Castles of the World", London, 1998
  • Cairns, C., "Castle of the West Highlands", Edinburgh, 2004
  • Mostard, E. and Heus, M. de, "Burchten en Kastelen", Lisse, 1999
  • Atrium cultuur gids "Kastelen", Alphen aan de Rijn, 1991
  • Barnes, I., "The historical atlas of knights and castles", London, 2007*
  • Kaufmann, J.E. and H.W., "The medieval fortress", Cambridge, 2001*

Castle books about a specific country or area

Asia

  • Turnbull, S., "Japanese Castles 1540–1640", Oxford, 2003*
  • Hein, D., "Festungen des Johanniterordens im Mittelmeer", Darmstadt, 2008*

Austria and Switzerland

  • Krahe, F.W., "Burgen des Deutschen Mittelalters", Augsburg, 1998
  • Clam, M., "Burgen und Schlösser in Österreich", Wien, 1991
  • Clasen, K.H., "Die mittelalterliche Kunst im Gebiete des Deutschordensstaates Preussen", 1927*

Benelux

  • Janssen, H.L. e.a., "1000 jaar kastelen in Nederland", Utrecht, 1996
  • Tromp, H., "Langs Nederlandse kastelen", Baarn, 1972
  • Haaren, P. van, "Kastelen in Zuid Holland en Zeeland", Rijswijk, 1999
  • Kransberg, D. and Mills, H., "Kastelen in Nederland", Amsterdam, 1980
  • De Groene Vakantiegids – Kastelen, 1990
  • Tromp, H., "Langs Belgische kastelen, van Motte tot Paleis", Baarn, 1980
  • Martens, J., "101 Belgische kastelen", Brussel, 1974
  • Meulenkamp, W. e.a., "Huizen van Stand", Alphen aan de Rijn, 1992*

Crusader castles

  • Müller-Wiener, W., "Castles of the Crusaders", London, 1966
  • Fedden, R., "Crusader Castles", London, 1950
  • Kennedy, H., "Crusader Castles", Cambridge, 1994
  • Boas, A.J., "Archaeology of the military orders", Abington, 2006*

Eastern Europe

  • Haris, A. e.a., "Historische Baudenkmäler in Ungarn", Budapest, 1999
  • Bodo, S., "Castles and Palaces of Hungary", Budapest, 1984
  • Fialová, V. e.a., "Hrady a Zámky", Praha, 1962
  • Vukadin, V. e.a., "Castles and Palaces of Slovenia", Ljubljana, 2002
  • Gotz, W. e.a., "Citadels of Transylvania", 1998*
  • Turnbull, S., "Crusader Castles of the Teutonic Knights", 2003*
  • Plaschka, R. e.a., "Österreichisches Ost- und Südosteuropa-Institut", 1986*

France

  • Erlande-Brandenburg, A., "L'Art Gothique", Liège, 2004
  • Mesqui, J., "Châteaux forts et fortifications en France", Paris, 1997
  • Salch, C.L., "Dictionnaire des Châteaux de l'Alsace médiévale", Strasbourg, 1978*
  • Poisson, M., "Châteaux de la Loire", Paris, 2005*

Germany

  • Krahe, F.W., "Burgen des Deutschen Mittelalters", Augsburg, 1998
  • Bingenheimer, K., "Die Luftschutzanlagen der Stadt Trier", Trier, 1996*
  • Mrusek, H.J., "Romanik", Leipzig, 1972*

Great Britain

  • Brown, R.A., "English Castles", London, 1954
  • Platt, C., "The Castle in Medieval England and Wales", London, 1982
  • Johnson, P., "Castles of England, Scotland and Wales", London, 1989
  • Cairns, C., "Castles of the West Highlands", Edinburgh, 2004
  • Bradbury, J., "The Medieval Siege", Suffolk, 1992*

Italy

  • Settia, A., "Rapine, assedi, battaglie", Bari, 2002*
  • De Vita, R., "Castelli, torri ed opere fortificate di Puglia", Bari, 1974*
  • Fedden, R. and Thompson, J., "Crusader Castles", London, 1957*

Spain

  • Paluzíe, A., "Great medieval castles of Spain", Barcelona, 2002
  • Martín, J.L. and Serrano-Piedecasas, L., "Tratado de Arquitectura Hispanomusulmana", Madrid, 2004*

Historical books and miscellaneous sources

  • Runciman, S., "A History of the Crusades", 3 vols, Cambridge, 1951–54
  • Riley-Smith, J., "The Atlas of the Crusades", London, 1991
  • Barber, M., "The Two Cities: Medieval Europe 1050-1320", 1992*
  • UNESCO World Heritage Site list
  • Game: Stronghold (FireFly Studios, 2001)

Castle types

Location types

Ebhardt [3] groups castles by the strategic advantages their location offers. The location and available building materials determine the unique character of a castle.

TypeDescriptionExample
Mountain castles (Höhenburgen) The approach to the castle is hampered by its high position. Siege engines cannot reach the castle. Schloß Vaduz
Rock castles (Felsenburgen) Castles built on top of a mountain rock. Parts of the castle, including its main entrance, can be inside the rock. The rock defends the castle against sapping and is considered to be the strongest castle type. Haut-Kœnigsbourg
Water castles (Wasserburgen) Surrounded by water in a moat or a lake. The water defends the castle against siege engines and sapping. In long sieges a moat can be filled with earth. Bodiam Castle
Harbour castles (Hafenburgen) One side is defended by water of a river or sea. Mostly used to defend a harbour. Château de Chillon
City castles (Stadtburgen) Castles built to defend or control a town. The castle can be shut off from the town. Because of its location against a town only one or two sides of the castle can be defended by height or water. Castle Limerick

Building structure types

Janssen [1] and Toy [2] both describe classifications of structure types. Eight building concepts are distinguished, differing in three aspects: the use of separate towers to defend the curtain walls; the presence of a donjon as a last defence tower; and the defensive position of the donjon — inside, in, or outside the curtain wall.

#ConceptDescriptionDrawingExample
1Motte-and-bailey A central tower or donjon on an artificial hill, defended by a curtain wall. Introduced by Scandinavian invaders in England and France in the 10th and 11th century. Gisors
2Later Norman A motte-and-bailey castle that is afterwards enclosed by or connected to a new curtain wall with flanking towers. Arundel, Windsor
3Ringwork / shell keep A round or multi-angular castle, located on a hill, without a separate tower or donjon and without towers in the curtain wall. Restormel
4Tower or compact castle Stand-alone tower or donjon, compact castles without a bailey, can be equipped with small flanking towers in the outer wall. Threave
5Rectangular or polygonal Rectangular or polygonal shaped castle with flanking towers in the curtain walls but without a central tower or donjon. Egeskov, Harlech
6Donjon inside curtain wall Rectangular shaped classical castle with flanking towers and a donjon positioned inside the curtain wall. The donjon cannot assist in defending the curtain walls directly and cannot be accessed from the curtain walls. Dover, Tower of London
7Donjon in curtain wall Rectangular shaped classical castle with flanking towers and a donjon positioned in the curtain wall. The donjon can assist in defending the curtain walls directly and can be accessed from the curtain walls. Kenilworth
8Donjon outside curtain wall Rectangular shaped classical castle with flanking towers and a donjon positioned outside the curtain wall. The donjon can assist in defending the curtain walls directly but cannot be accessed from the curtain walls. Bellver

The present condition of the castle

ConditionDescriptionExample
Intact The castle is mostly intact; all characteristic parts (gate, donjon, moat, defence works) are intact and accessible. Corroy le Château
Rebuild / Restored The castle appears intact but some or all characteristic parts have been rebuilt or restored. Pierrefonds
Damaged The castle is not intact but the characteristic parts (gate, donjon, moat, defence works) are still recognisable. Kasteel Teylingen
Ruined / Partly remained Some characteristic parts (gate, donjon, moat, defence works) are lost. Some parts like the donjon can still be intact, rebuilt or restored. Ruïne van Brederode
Destroyed All characteristic parts (gate, donjon, moat, defence works) are lost. Château de Bastille

Literature

  • [1] Janssen, H.L. e.a., "1000 jaar kastelen in Nederland", Utrecht, 1996
  • [2] Toy, S., "Castles, their construction and history", New York, 1985
  • [3] Ebhardt, B., "Der Wehrbau Europas im Mittelalter", Berlin, 1939

Websites about castles

Per country

CountryWebsite
Belgiumkastelenbelgie.be
Czech Republichrady.cz
Englandcastles-abbeys.co.uk
Germanyburgenwelt.de
Netherlandskasteleninnederland.nl
Walescastlewales.com

General castle sites

  • kasteleninnederland.nl
  • castles.org
  • castles.nl

Castle books

General

  • Coppin, B., "Castles, Defending King and Country", Harpenden, 2006
  • Zeune, J., "Burgen, Symbole der Macht", Regensburg, 1997
  • Gravett, C., "The History of Castles", Guilford, 2001
  • Brown, R. Allen, "Castles, a history and guide", Poole, 1980
  • Toy, S., "Castles, their construction and history", New York, 1985

Per country

  • Johnson, P., "Castles of England, Scotland and Wales", London, 1989
  • Mesqui, J., "Châteaux forts et fortifications en France", Paris, 1997
  • Janssen, H.L. e.a., "1000 jaar kastelen in Nederland", Utrecht, 1996
  • Tromp, H., "Langs Belgische kastelen, van Motte tot Paleis", Baarn, 1980
  • Paluzíe, A., "Great medieval castles of Spain", Barcelona, 2002

Contributed pictures

Photographers

Many thanks to these photographers who have contributed their castle pictures to this site:

  • Bart Flos (Nicola)
  • Toine Caarls (Bunratty, Limerick)
  • Nicole van den Wildenberg (Agra, Fatehpur, Jaipur, Rhodes)
  • Kristiaan de Vries (Almere)
  • Jan-Willem van Helvoirt (Brodick, Edinburgh, Eilean Donan)
  • Frans Maas (Malbork)
  • Max Donk (Saumur, Rosendaal en Doesburg)
  • Rob Verschoor (Bodrum, Eilean Donan, Urquhart)
  • John Wennips (Krak, Oostvoorne, Dunnotar, Chastel Rouge, Balduinseck)
  • Bram Meijer (Carcassonne)
  • Mariska Rongen (Oystermouth)
  • Stéphane William Gondoin (Plessis-Bourré, Dijon, Sully, Foix, Arques-la-Bataille, Ussé)
  • Françoise Collignon (Fougères)
  • Jeroen Vos (Kalmar, Örebro, Gripsholm, Olavsborg, Gruyères, Tavastehus, Luck Slott, Tower)
  • Tonny Lie (Bran)
  • Castle Area Campaign (Clifford)
  • Jos Schreur (Almodóvar, Alhambra, Castillo de Baños de la Encina)
  • Vincent van Rooij (Valkenburg)
  • Ron Lyons (Alnwick, Bodiam, Hever, Middleham, and many more)
  • Jaap van der Lijn (Alnwick, Bamburgh, Braemar, Doune, Urquhart)
  • Hevesi Zoltán (Diósgyőr, Csesznek, Nagyvázson)
  • Csonti Csilla (Sümeg)
  • Pinkert László (Oravský hrad, Ľubovniansky hrad)
  • László János (Csesznek, Diósgyőr, Salgó, Sárospatak, Sümeg, Visegrád)

propertiesClass.name: snippets

components.templateData:
Data Access & Developer Guide

The TopCastles dataset is openly available as static JSON, an OpenAPI-described REST API, and an MCP server for direct AI assistant integration. No authentication, no rate limits, no sign-up required.

What's available

api REST / JSON API Static pre-sliced files Zero-infrastructure static JSON endpoints. Fetch all castles, the top 100, or filter by country. CORS-friendly, cacheable. smart_toy MCP Server Model Context Protocol Connect any MCP-compatible AI assistant (Claude Desktop, Cursor, VS Code Copilot) and query the dataset conversationally. schema Data Schema OpenAPI 3.0 + field reference Full OpenAPI 3.0 specification plus a field-by-field reference for the Castle object including Wikipedia and Wikidata enrichment fields. psychology For AI Agents llms.txt & discovery An llms.txt discovery file lets any LLM self-discover the dataset. Includes example prompts and query patterns for agents.

Dataset at a glance

Castles1,000 ranked entries
Countries56 countries
Wikipedia enrichment~785 entries with extract, thumbnail, URL
Wikidata enrichment~777 entries with heritage status, style, inception year
CoordinatesLatitude / longitude for most castles
LicenseCreative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 3.0
FormatJSON (UTF-8)

Quick start

Fetch the top 10 castles in three lines:

{{ snippets['quickstart'] }}

Static JSON Endpoints

All endpoints are pre-generated static files served directly from the same origin. No server-side processing. Responses are cacheable and work from any language or tool.

GET /api/index.json API index and country list

Returns the API manifest: total castle count, all available endpoints, and the full list of country slugs with castle counts.

{{ snippets['curlIndex'] }}
GET /api/castles.json All 1,000 castles

Complete dataset sorted by score_total descending. Includes all enrichment fields.

{{ snippets['curlAll'] }}
GET /api/top100.json Top 100 castles by score

The 100 highest-scoring castles. Useful when you only need the most prominent entries.

{{ snippets['curlTop100'] }}
GET /api/by-country/{slug}.json Castles for one country

Returns all castles in a specific country, sorted by score. Slugs are lowercase hyphenated country names — full list in /api/index.json. 

{{ snippets['curlCountry'] }}
GET /api/openapi.yaml OpenAPI 3.0 specification

Machine-readable API description. Import into Postman, Insomnia, or any OpenAPI-compatible tool.

download Download openapi.yaml

Usage examples

JavaScript / fetch

{{ snippets['jsExample'] }}

Python

{{ snippets['pyExample'] }}

Model Context Protocol Server

The TopCastles MCP server lets any MCP-compatible AI assistant query the castle dataset directly — without copy-pasting data. Once connected, you can ask things like "Find intact 12th-century hilltop castles in France" and get structured results.

check_circle Claude Desktop check_circle Cursor check_circle VS Code Copilot check_circle Any MCP client

Setup

  1. Use the hosted MCP server URL 
    {{ snippets['mcpUrl'] }}
  2. Configure your AI assistant

    Add the hosted URL to claude_desktop_config.json (Claude Desktop) or your tool's MCP config:

    {{ snippets['mcpConfig'] }}
  3. Restart your AI assistant — the topcastles tools will appear automatically.

Available tools

search_castles Search and filter across the full dataset

Search and filter castles. All parameters are optional.

ParameterTypeDescription
querystringText search across name, description, remarkable, founder, Wikipedia extract
countrystringFilter by country name (case-insensitive)
eraintegerCentury of construction (e.g. 12 for 12th century)
conditionstringe.g. "Intact", "Ruined/Partly remained"
castle_typestringe.g. "Hilltop castle", "Water castle"
heritagebooleanIf true, only castles with a heritage designation
limitintegerMax results (default 10, max 100)
 
{{ snippets['toolSearch'] }}
 get_castle Full detail for one castle by code

Returns all fields including the full Wikipedia extract and Wikidata enrichment.

ParameterTypeDescription
castle_codestringThe castle identifier, e.g. "krak-des-chevaliers"
 
{{ snippets['toolGet'] }}
 list_countries All countries with castle counts

Returns all 56 countries with their castle count and the top-scoring castle per country. No parameters.

{{ snippets['toolCountries'] }}
 nearby_castles Castles nearest to a coordinate

Computes Haversine distance from the given point and returns the closest castles.

ParameterTypeDescription
latitudenumberWGS84 latitude
longitudenumberWGS84 longitude
limitintegerResults to return (default 5, max 20)
max_kmnumberOptional maximum distance in kilometres
 
{{ snippets['toolNearby'] }}

Example conversations

"What are the top 10 castles in Germany?"
"Find intact crusader castles from the 12th century"
"Which castles are UNESCO World Heritage Sites?"
"What castles are within 50 km of Vienna?"
"Tell me everything about Krak des Chevaliers"
"Which country has the most castles in the dataset?"

Data Schema

Every castle record is a flat JSON object. Fields from Wikipedia and Wikidata enrichment are nullable — not all castles have a Wikipedia article or Wikidata entry.

download Download OpenAPI 3.0 spec (YAML)

Core fields

FieldTypeDescription
castle_codestringUnique identifier and URL slug (e.g. "krak-des-chevaliers")
castle_namestringCastle name
positioninteger?Rank in the dataset (1 = highest score)
score_totalnumber?Composite score (sources + visitor rating)
score_visitorsnumber?Visitor rating 0–10
visitorsinteger?Number of visitor ratings
countrystringCountry name
regionstringRegion name
region_codestringRegion slug
placestringNearest town or village
areastringComma-separated area / theme tags
latitudenumber?WGS84 latitude
longitudenumber?WGS84 longitude
erainteger?Century of construction (12 = 12th century)
castle_typestringLocation type
castle_conceptstringStructural concept
conditionstringPreservation state
founderstringBuilder, dynasty, or military order
remarkablestringNotable feature summary
descriptionstringShort editorial description
websitestringOfficial or reference URL

Wikipedia enrichment

FieldTypeDescription
wikipedia_extractstring?First paragraph from the Wikipedia article
wikipedia_urlstring?Full URL to the Wikipedia article
wikipedia_thumbnailstring?URL of the Wikipedia thumbnail image

Wikidata enrichment

FieldTypeWikidata propertyDescription
wikidata_idstring?—Wikidata QID (e.g. "Q23053")
architectural_stylestring?P149Architectural style label
heritage_statusstring?P1435Heritage designation (e.g. "UNESCO World Heritage Site")
inception_yearinteger?P571Year of construction
wikidata_imagestring?P18Wikimedia Commons image URL

Enumerated values

castle_type

@for (v of castleTypes; track v) { {{ v }} }

condition

@for (v of conditions; track v) { {{ v }} }

For AI Agents & LLMs

TopCastles follows the llms.txt convention so any LLM can self-discover the dataset without manual configuration.

Discovery file

A machine-readable discovery file is served at /llms.txt. It describes the dataset, all endpoints, the Castle schema, and example query patterns.

open_in_new View /llms.txt

Recommended access pattern

  1. Discover: Fetch /llms.txt or /api/index.json to learn available endpoints and schema.
  2. Fetch data: Use the most specific endpoint. For country queries use /api/by-country/{slug}.json rather than loading all 1,000 castles.
  3. Filter client-side: Era, condition, and type filtering is done after fetching — the static API has no query parameters.
  4. Or use MCP: The MCP server provides server-side filtering via search_castles — no client-side logic needed.

Example agent queries

"What are the top castles in France?" 
{{ snippets['agentFrance'] }}
 "Find UNESCO World Heritage castles" 
{{ snippets['agentUnesco'] }}
 "Which castles are near Paris?" 
{{ snippets['agentNearby'] }}
 "Which country has the most castles?" 
{{ snippets['agentCountry'] }}

Data freshness

The generated field in /api/index.json shows the last enrichment date. Agents can poll this to detect stale data.

License

Dataset published under Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial 3.0. Attribution: TopCastles (topcastles.com) by Robert Rongen. Commercial use requires separate permission.

components.name: HomePageComponent
components.templateData:
The top 1000 of medieval castles

In Europe and Asia more than 10,000 medieval castles can be found. Topcastles.com selected the most beautiful and interesting castles and ranked them in a top 1000 list. The construction of this list is explained in the menu background. The total list contains almost 1,000 castles. This makes it possible to show toplists per country or region, this can be helpful for planning your holiday castle trips. These are the top twelve of the list:

Top twelve by visitor rating

Every square picture of a castle on this site links to the information page of that castle. The castles with the highest ratings are listed separately in the visitors top 1000. The pictures below represent the top twelve castles of this list:

Lists per country, castle type and more

In "All Castles" you can search the database and create your own list of topcastles. "Per country" contains a selection of topcastles per country and area and even a list of top castle countries and regions. "Castle types" lists the topcastles per castle type. The pictures below represent the top twelve castles of the Netherlands:

components.name: Top100PageComponent

propertiesClass.name: conceptDescriptions
propertiesClass.defaultValue:
{
    'Motte-and-bailey': { description: 'A central tower on an artificial hill, defended by a curtain wall.', image: 'concept_motte.jpg' },
    'Later norman': { description: 'A motte-and-bailey enclosed by a new curtain wall with flanking towers.', image: 'concept_gisors.jpg' },
    'Ringwork': { description: 'Or shell keep: A round castle on a hill, without a separate tower or donjon.', image: 'castle_concept2.jpg' },
    'Tower or compact castle': { description: 'Stand-alone tower or donjon, compact castle without a bailey.', image: 'castle_concept4.jpg' },
    'Rectangular or polygonal': { description: 'Rectangular or polygonal castle with flanking towers but without a central tower.', image: 'castle_concept3.jpg' },
    'Donjon inside curtain wall': { description: 'Classical castle with flanking towers and a central donjon inside the curtain wall.', image: 'castle_concept5.jpg' },
    'Donjon in curtain wall': { description: 'Classical castle with a central donjon positioned in the curtain wall.', image: 'concept_kenilworth.jpg' },
    'Donjon outside curtain wall': { description: 'Classical castle with a central donjon positioned outside the curtain wall.', image: 'concept_bellver.jpg' },
  }

propertiesClass.name: conditionDescriptions
propertiesClass.defaultValue:
{
    'Intact': { description: 'All characteristic parts (gate, donjon, moat, defence works) are intact and accessible.' },
    'Rebuild/Restored': { description: 'Appears intact but some parts have been rebuilt or restored.' },
    'Damaged': { description: 'Not intact but characteristic parts are still recognisable.' },
    'Ruined/Partly remained': { description: 'Some characteristic parts are lost; some like the donjon can still be intact.' },
    'Destroyed': { description: 'All characteristic parts are lost.' },
  }

propertiesClass.name: favoriteCodes
filterFields
propertiesClass.defaultValue:
[
    { key: 'country',        label: 'Country' },
    { key: 'region',         label: 'Region' },
    { key: 'castle_type',    label: 'Location Type' },
    { key: 'castle_concept', label: 'Building Concept' },
    { key: 'condition',      label: 'Condition' },
  ]

propertiesClass.name: searchedCastles
typeDescriptions
propertiesClass.defaultValue:
{
    'Mountain castle': { description: 'Höhenburgen: The approach to the castle is hampered by its high position. Siege engines cannot reach the castle.' },
    'Rock castle': { description: 'Felsenburgen: Castles built on top of a mountain rock. Parts of the castle can be inside the rock. The Wasgau border region is famous for rock castles.' },
    'Water castle': { description: 'Wasserburgen: Surrounded by water in a moat or lake. The water defends against siege engines and sapping.' },
    'Harbour castle': { description: 'Hafenburgen: One side defended by water of a river or sea. Mostly used to defend a harbour.' },
    'City castle': { description: 'Stadtburgen: Castles built to defend or control a town. Can be shut off from the town.' },
  }

components.name: ViewToggleComponent

inputsClass.name: hideMap
inputsClass.description: When true, hides the map toggle (use when the host page has its own map panel).
inputsClass.rawdescription: When true, hides the map toggle (use when the host page has its own map panel).

components.templateData:
<mat-button-toggle-group
  [value]="viewModeService.mode()"
  (change)="setView($event.value)"
  aria-label="View mode">
  <mat-button-toggle value="list" aria-label="List view">
    <mat-icon>view_list</mat-icon>
  </mat-button-toggle>
  <mat-button-toggle value="grid" aria-label="Grid view">
    <mat-icon>grid_view</mat-icon>
  </mat-button-toggle>
  @if (!hideMap()) {
    <mat-button-toggle value="map" aria-label="Map view">
      <mat-icon>map</mat-icon>
    </mat-button-toggle>
  }
</mat-button-toggle-group>

variables.name: routes
variables.defaultValue:
[
  { path: '', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/home/home-page.component').then(m => m.HomePageComponent)" },
  // Main castle list — supports ?country=, ?region=, ?castleType=, ?condition= query params
  { path: 'top1000', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/top100/top100-page.component').then(m => m.Top100PageComponent)" },
  // Castle detail
  { path: 'castles/:code', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/castle-detail/castle-detail-page.component').then(m => m.CastleDetailPageComponent)" },
  // Redirects for phased-out pages
  { path: 'castles', redirectTo: '/top1000', pathMatch: 'full' },
  { path: 'countries/:country', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/country-redirect/country-redirect.component').then(m => m.CountryRedirectComponent)" },
  // Background / informational pages
  { path: 'top-countries', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/top-countries/top-countries-page.component').then(m => m.TopCountriesPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'top-regions', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/top-regions/top-regions-page.component').then(m => m.TopRegionsPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'nocastle/:code', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/nocastle-detail/nocastle-detail-page.component').then(m => m.NoCastleDetailPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'background', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/background/background-page.component').then(m => m.BackgroundPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'developer', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/developer/developer-page.component').then(m => m.DeveloperPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'favorites', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/favorites/favorites-page.component').then(m => m.FavoritesPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'favorites/:id', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/favorites-detail/favorites-detail-page.component').then(m => m.FavoritesDetailPageComponent)" },
  { path: '**', redirectTo: '' },
]

  // Main castle list — supports ?country=, ?region=, ?castleType=, ?condition= query params

  // Redirects for phased-out pages

  { path: 'countries/:country', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/country-redirect/country-redirect.component').then(m => m.CountryRedirectComponent)" },
  // Background / informational pages
  { path: 'top-countries', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/top-countries/top-countries-page.component').then(m => m.TopCountriesPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'top-regions', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/top-regions/top-regions-page.component').then(m => m.TopRegionsPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'nocastle/:code', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/nocastle-detail/nocastle-detail-page.component').then(m => m.NoCastleDetailPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'background', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/background/background-page.component').then(m => m.BackgroundPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'developer', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/developer/developer-page.component').then(m => m.DeveloperPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'favorites', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/favorites/favorites-page.component').then(m => m.FavoritesPageComponent)" },
  { path: 'favorites/:id', loadComponent: () => "import('./pages/favorites-detail/favorites-detail-page.component').then(m => m.FavoritesDetailPageComponent)" },
  { path: '**', redirectTo: '' },
]
